does: MedBench v5 introduces a dynamic, process-oriented evaluation framework for clinical multimodal AI systems that moves beyond static question-answering benchmarks. The framework combines Clinical Cognitive Responsiveness with 14 sub-dimensions and Medical Atomic Skills across 4 agent environments, covering 63 tasks total. The benchmark incorporates three information-flow stressors—omission, contradiction, and evidence delay—to analyze how models degrade under realistic clinical conditions. Critically, it implements a dynamic process audit protocol with five reasoning nodes that generates model-specific failure fingerprints and tracks hallucination propagation through initiation, anchoring, and contradiction interaction stages. Experiments on frontier models reveal that strong aggregate task performance masks process instability, with stressors particularly disrupting contradiction detection, diagnosis updating, and hallucination handling.
connects: This work builds on a decade of medical AI benchmarking efforts that have progressively moved from simple accuracy metrics toward more nuanced evaluation. It responds to recognized limitations in existing benchmarks like MedQA, PubMedQA, and earlier MedBench versions that focus on static task performance without examining reasoning processes or failure modes. The paper extends work on hallucination detection in large language models, which has become increasingly important as models are deployed in high-stakes domains. It connects to research on adversarial robustness and stress-testing of AI systems, as well as process-oriented evaluation methods from AI interpretability literature. The focus on atomic skill decomposition and agent-based evaluation reflects growing recognition that clinical tasks require systematic reasoning and tool use beyond text generation.
enables: This benchmark enables more rigorous validation of clinical AI systems before deployment and supports targeted improvements in model reliability. The failure fingerprint approach allows developers to identify specific weaknesses in reasoning chains rather than just accuracy gaps, enabling more efficient model refinement. The hallucination propagation monitoring framework can drive development of mechanisms to prevent and detect silent errors that might otherwise propagate through clinical decision chains. The stressor-based analysis provides a foundation for studying clinical robustness, potentially informing which clinical contexts require additional safeguards. This work opens directions for adaptive benchmarking that tracks how models improve on specific process dimensions, development of hallucination-resistant architectures for medical tasks, and integration of such evaluation into continuous monitoring systems for deployed clinical AI applications.
